text.title: Hourly Rate Floor Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every rate-floor calculation must enforce. These prevent the most common pricing mistake: dividing target income by 2080 hours.

rule.statement: Use billable utilization between 40% and 60% of available work hours. Reject any calculation that uses 100% utilization or unstated utilization. Default if no operator data: 50%.

rationale.source: Brennan Dunn / Jonathan Stark casework, agency benchmarks

rule.rationale: Real solo practices spend 40-60% of work hours on non-billable activities (sales, admin, learning, dead leads, sick days). 100%-utilization math systematically undercuts the floor by 1.7-2.5×.

rule.statement: Gross up target take-home income by jurisdiction-specific effective tax rate INCLUDING self-employment tax / NI / VAT-on-services if applicable. Forbidden: using marginal rate as effective rate.
rule.rationale: In the US, self-employment tax adds ~15.3% on top of income tax. In the UK, NI Class 4 adds ~9%. Operators using marginal rate alone under-gross-up by 8-15% of revenue.

rule.statement: Add 12 months of business overhead (tools, insurance, healthcare, training, office, accounting) to the grossed-up income BEFORE dividing by billable hours. Healthcare and pension contributions are mandatory when no employer covers them.
rule.rationale: Forgotten overhead averages $8k-$25k per year for typical solo dev / consultant. Excluding it sinks the floor below sustainability within 2 years.

rule.statement: Produce three numbers: bottom (walk-away threshold), floor (sustainable target), stretch (target with margin for investment + savings + bad-debt reserve). Reject single-number outputs.
rule.rationale: A single number anchors the operator to the wrong value during negotiation. Three bands give the operator a clear floor and a stretch goal.

rule.statement: The calculation uses the operator's tax + healthcare + insurance jurisdiction, NOT the client's jurisdiction. A Portuguese freelancer billing a US client uses Portuguese effective rates.
rule.rationale: Operators charging "US rates" but paying European taxes mistakenly assume they're earning US wages. Floor is anchored to the operator's actual tax + cost-of-living position.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.

example.title: Floor calculation with three bands
example.code:
Inputs: Target take-home (annual): $80,000 Effective tax rate (US, solo): 32% (income + SE tax) Required gross income: $80,000 / (1 - 0.32) = $117,647 Annual overhead (12 mo total): $14,000 (tools $3k, healthcare $9k, accounting $2k) Required revenue: $117,647 + $14,000 = $131,647 Available work hours/year: 2,000 (50 weeks × 40) Utilization: 50% (1,000 billable hours)
Output bands: bottom: $115/hr (covers basic survival at 40% utilization) floor: $132/hr (target sustainable at 50% utilization) stretch: $165/hr (margin for savings + business reinvestment)
example.note: Illustrates r1-r5 applied. Three bands give the operator pricing flexibility.

example.title: 2080-hour fantasy math
example.code: Naive calc: $80,000 / 2080 hrs = $38/hr (rate quoted) Reality: at 50% utilization, $38/hr × 1000 billable hrs = $38,000 gross, minus taxes ~$12k, minus overhead ~$14k = $12,000 take-home. 15% of target.
example.note: Illustrates r1 + r3 violation. 2080-hour math is the dominant solo-pricing failure mode.
